_comment: Company Troubleshooting Knowledge Base — mock data for the Triage Agent tool.
_note: Add new entries following the same schema. The agent will search by error_code and keyword matching.

entries.title: HTTP 500 – Internal Server Error
entries.description: A catch-all for any server-side failure that doesn't map to a more specific error code.
common_causes: Unhandled exception in application code
Out-of-memory (OOM) kill — container was evicted
Database connection pool exhausted
Downstream service timeout bubbled up
Misconfigured environment variable (missing secret)
action_items: Check application logs: kubectl logs <pod> --previous
Inspect OOMKill events: kubectl describe pod <pod>
Verify database connectivity from within the pod
Review recent deployments for config or code changes
Escalate to Senior Dev if root cause is not identified within 30 min

entries.title: HTTP 503 – Service Unavailable
entries.description: The service cannot handle the request, typically because it is down, overloaded, or mid-restart.
common_causes: All pods are in CrashLoopBackOff or Pending state
Readiness probe failing — pod exists but not receiving traffic
HPA has not yet scaled up to handle a traffic spike
Upstream dependency (cache, DB, third-party API) is down
Node pressure causing pod eviction
action_items: Check pod status: kubectl get pods -n <namespace>
Inspect readiness probe: kubectl describe pod <pod>
Check HPA status: kubectl get hpa
Verify upstream services are healthy
Restart Deployment if pods are stuck: kubectl rollout restart deployment/<name>

entries.title: HTTP 403 – Forbidden
entries.description: The client is authenticated but lacks the permission to perform the requested action.
common_causes: Expired or revoked API token / certificate
Missing or incorrect RBAC role binding in Kubernetes
IAM policy change that removed a required permission
Wrong service account attached to the Pod
IP allowlist or network policy blocking the source
action_items: Verify the token / credential is valid and not expired
Review RBAC: kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource> --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa>
Check recent IAM / policy changes in audit logs
Confirm the correct ServiceAccount is mounted in the Pod spec
Do NOT restart the pod — this is an auth issue, not a process issue

entries.title: HTTP 401 – Unauthorized
entries.description: The request lacks valid authentication credentials.
common_causes: JWT token has expired (check exp claim)
Wrong audience (aud) or issuer (iss) in token
Secret rotation completed but pod still holds old token
Kubernetes Secret not correctly mounted or projected
action_items: Decode and inspect the JWT: jwt.io or `kubectl exec` into pod
Rotate the secret and trigger a rolling restart of affected pods
Verify the Secret is correctly referenced in the Deployment env vars
Check identity provider / OAuth2 issuer logs for clues

entries.title: HTTP 404 – Not Found
entries.description: The requested resource or route does not exist on the server.
common_causes: Typo in the URL or incorrect API version prefix
Service route removed or renamed in a recent deployment
Ingress / route rule misconfiguration
Feature flag disabled the endpoint
action_items: Confirm the correct endpoint path in the API documentation
Check Ingress / Route rules: kubectl get ingress -n <namespace>
Review recent deployment diff for removed routes
Verify feature flags if your platform supports them

entries.title: Database Connection Refused
entries.description: The application cannot establish a TCP connection to the database.
common_causes: Database pod / StatefulSet is down or being rescheduled
Wrong DB_HOST or DB_PORT in application config
Network Policy blocking egress from app pod to DB pod
Database connection pool limit reached (max_connections exceeded)
Database volume is full (no space left on device)
action_items: Check DB pod status: kubectl get pods -l app=<db-label> -n <namespace>
Test connectivity from app pod: kubectl exec -it <pod> -- nc -zv <db-host> <db-port>
Verify DB_HOST / DB_PORT env vars in the app deployment
Review NetworkPolicy objects for egress rules
Check DB logs for connection limit errors or disk-full messages

entries.title: Out-of-Memory (OOMKilled)
entries.description: The container exceeded its memory limit and was terminated by the Linux kernel OOM killer.
common_causes: Memory limit set too low for the workload
Memory leak in application code
Unexpected traffic spike causing heap growth
Large payload being buffered entirely in memory
action_items: Confirm OOMKill: kubectl describe pod <pod> | grep -i oom
Review memory usage trends in Prometheus / Grafana
Temporarily increase memory limit in the Deployment spec
Profile the application for memory leaks (pprof, py-spy, etc.)
Implement streaming/pagination to avoid large in-memory buffers

entries.title: Request Timeout / Gateway Timeout
entries.description: A request exceeded the configured time limit somewhere in the call chain.
common_causes: Slow downstream service (DB query, external API)
Pod CPU throttling — request processing is starved
Large synchronous payload — should be async
Kubernetes liveness probe timeout too aggressive, causing restart loop
action_items: Identify the slowest span using distributed tracing (Jaeger / Tempo)
Check CPU throttling: kubectl top pods -n <namespace>
Review and tune timeout values in Ingress / service mesh config
Consider moving long-running work to an async queue (e.g. Kafka, SQS)
Escalate to Senior Dev if the bottleneck is in a third-party service

entries.title: CrashLoopBackOff
entries.description: Kubernetes is repeatedly restarting a container because it exits immediately after starting.
common_causes: Application fails startup due to missing required environment variable
Liveness probe too aggressive — kills pod before it is ready
Application crash on startup (bad config file, missing secret)
Image entrypoint / CMD is wrong
action_items: Read crash logs: kubectl logs <pod> --previous
Describe the pod for probe and event details: kubectl describe pod <pod>
Check all required env vars are present (compare .env.example vs ConfigMap/Secret)
Temporarily set restartPolicy to Never to capture the full crash output
Escalate to Senior Dev with full logs if the exit code is non-obvious

entries.title: Pod Pending / Scheduling Failure
entries.description: A pod stays in Pending because the scheduler cannot place it on any node.
common_causes: Insufficient CPU or memory on nodes for requested requests/limits
Pod nodeSelector or affinity rules match no nodes
Taints on nodes without matching tolerations
PersistentVolumeClaim not bound or zone mismatch
Cluster autoscaler not scaling or max node pool reached
action_items: Describe the pod: kubectl describe pod <pod> -n <namespace> (check Events)
Check node capacity: kubectl describe nodes | grep -A5 Allocated
Review requests/limits vs available resources on the node pool
Verify PVC status: kubectl get pvc -n <namespace>
Relax affinity/selector or add tolerations if policy allows

entries.title: ImagePullBackOff / ErrImagePull
entries.description: Kubernetes cannot pull the container image referenced by the pod spec.
common_causes: Wrong image tag or repository name in the Deployment
Missing imagePullSecrets for a private registry
Registry credentials expired or RBAC denied pull
Image deleted from registry or air-gapped cluster without mirror
action_items: Describe pod events: kubectl describe pod <pod> -n <namespace>
Verify image name:tag in Deployment matches registry
Confirm imagePullSecrets on the ServiceAccount or pod spec
Test pull manually from a debug pod or crane/skopeo if allowed
Roll back Deployment if a bad image was pushed in the last release

entries.title: Insufficient Cluster Resources
entries.description: Workloads cannot run because the cluster or namespace lacks allocatable capacity.
common_causes: Namespace ResourceQuota limits reached
Node pool at capacity — pending pods accumulate
LimitRange prevents scheduling due to default limits
Noisy neighbor workloads consuming node resources
action_items: List pending pods and events: kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase=Pending
Check namespace quota: kubectl describe resourcequota -n <namespace>
Review node allocatable vs requests: kubectl top nodes (if metrics available)
Scale node pool or reduce workload requests temporarily
Escalate to platform team if cluster-wide saturation persists
